topics.title: Categories
topics.content: Security control categories define how controls are implemented and managed within an organization. They help classify security measures based on their nature and responsibility.

items.name: Technical
items.description: Controls implemented using hardware or software to protect systems, networks, and data from unauthorized access or attacks.

items.name: Managerial
items.description: Controls focused on governance, policies, procedures, and risk management decisions. These controls define how security is managed at an organizational level.

items.name: Operational
items.description: Controls carried out by people through processes and procedures. These ensure day-to-day security operations are effective.

items.name: Physical
items.description: Controls designed to protect physical assets, facilities, and infrastructure from unauthorized access or damage.

quiz.question: Which category includes firewalls and encryption?
quiz.explanation: Technical controls rely on hardware and software mechanisms.

quiz.question: Security policies and governance belong to which category?
quiz.explanation: Managerial controls define rules and risk management strategies.

quiz.question: An incident response procedure is an example of:
quiz.explanation: Operational controls are performed by people.

quiz.question: Security cameras and guards are part of:
quiz.explanation: They protect physical assets and locations.

quiz.question: Which category focuses on risk assessment and compliance?
quiz.explanation: Risk management is a managerial responsibility.

topics.title: Control Types
topics.content: Control types define the purpose of a control in preventing, detecting, or responding to security threats. They describe how a control behaves in the security lifecycle.

items.name: Preventive
items.description: Controls that stop security incidents before they occur.

items.name: Deterrent
items.description: Controls that discourage attackers from attempting an attack.

items.name: Detective
items.description: Controls that identify and alert on security incidents after they occur.

items.name: Corrective
items.description: Controls that fix or restore systems after an incident.

items.name: Compensating
items.description: Alternative controls used when a primary control is not feasible.

items.name: Directive
items.description: Controls that provide guidance and enforce policies or procedures.

quiz.question: A firewall is an example of which control type?
quiz.explanation: It blocks unauthorized access before it happens.

quiz.question: A visible security camera that discourages theft is:
An IDS that alerts on suspicious activity is:
quiz.explanation: It identifies attacks after they occur.

quiz.question: Restoring data from backups is:
quiz.explanation: It restores systems after an incident.

quiz.question: Using MFA because passwords alone are weak is:
quiz.explanation: It compensates for a weak primary control.

quiz.question: Which control type is BEST for reducing the likelihood of an attack?
quiz.explanation: Preventive controls stop attacks before they occur.

quiz.question: Which control type is used to identify a breach in progress?

topics.title: CIA Triad
topics.content: The CIA triad represents the three core principles of information security: confidentiality, integrity, and availability.

items.name: Confidentiality
items.description: Ensures that data is only accessible to authorized users.

items.name: Integrity
items.description: Ensures data is accurate, consistent, and not modified improperly.

items.name: Availability
items.description: Ensures systems and data are accessible when needed.

quiz.question: Hashing ensures:
quiz.explanation: It verifies data has not been altered.

topics.title: Non-repudiation
topics.content: Non-repudiation ensures that a user cannot deny performing an action.

items.name: Non-repudiation
items.description: Provides proof of origin and integrity so actions cannot be denied.

quiz.question: Which technology provides non-repudiation?
quiz.explanation: Digital signatures prove identity and origin.

topics.title: AAA
topics.content: AAA stands for Authentication, Authorization, and Accounting, which together control access and track activity.

items.name: Authentication
items.description: Verifies the identity of users or systems.

items.name: Authenticating people
items.description: Methods used to verify a human user's identity (something you know/are/have/do/where you are).

items.name: Authenticating systems
items.description: Methods used to verify devices/services (machine identity) before allowing access.

items.name: Authorization
items.description: Determines what actions a user is allowed to perform.

items.name: Authorization models
items.description: Ways to define and enforce access decisions based on roles, attributes, or rules.

topics.title: Gap Analysis
topics.content: Gap analysis identifies the difference between current security posture and desired state.

items.name: Gap Analysis
items.description: Compares current controls with required controls to identify missing protections.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of gap analysis?

topics.title: Zero Trust
topics.content: Zero Trust is a security model that assumes no implicit trust and requires continuous verification.

items.name: Adaptive identity
items.description: Adjusts authentication requirements based on risk.

items.name: Threat scope reduction
items.description: Reduces the blast radius of an attacker by limiting access paths and exposure.

items.name: Policy-driven access control
items.description: Access decisions are made dynamically based on context and policies instead of static network location.

items.name: Policy Administrator
items.description: Enforces policies from the policy engine.

items.name: Implicit trust zones
items.description: Traditional 'trusted' network areas assumed safe; Zero Trust aims to eliminate these assumptions.

topics.title: Physical Security
topics.content: Physical security protects facilities and infrastructure.

items.name: Access control vestibule
items.description: Mantrap-style entry area to prevent tailgating and enforce access control.

items.name: Sensors
items.description: Detect physical intrusion attempts and trigger alerts.

items.name: Microwave
items.description: Uses microwave signals to detect motion.

items.name: Ultrasonic
items.description: Uses ultrasonic waves to detect movement.

topics.title: Deception Technologies
topics.content: Deception technologies are used to mislead attackers and detect malicious activity.

items.name: Honeyfile
items.description: Fake file used to detect access.

items.name: Honeytoken
items.description: Fake data used to detect misuse.

topics.title: Business Processes Impacting Security Operation
topics.content: Change management ensures that any modification to systems, applications, or infrastructure is controlled, tested, and approved to minimize security risks and operational disruptions.

items.name: Approval process
items.description: Formal authorization required before implementing any change to ensure it has been reviewed and validated.

items.name: Ownership
items.description: Defines who is responsible for implementing and maintaining the change.

items.name: Stakeholders
items.description: Individuals or teams affected by the change who must be informed or consulted.

items.name: Impact analysis
items.description: Assessment of how a change affects systems, security, and operations.

items.name: Test results
items.description: Validation of the change in a controlled environment before deployment.

items.name: Backout plan
items.description: Procedure to revert changes if they fail or introduce issues.

items.name: Maintenance window
items.description: Scheduled time period to perform changes with minimal impact.

items.name: Standard operating procedure
items.description: Documented step-by-step instructions for implementing changes consistently.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of an approval process?
quiz.explanation: Approval ensures changes are validated before implementation.

quiz.question: What does impact analysis evaluate?
quiz.explanation: It evaluates how the change affects operations and security.

quiz.question: Why is a backout plan critical?
quiz.explanation: It allows recovery if the change fails.

quiz.question: Who must be informed about changes?
quiz.explanation: Stakeholders are affected by the change.

quiz.question: Why use a maintenance window?
quiz.explanation: Changes are done during low-impact periods.

topics.title: Technical Implications
topics.content: Changes can affect system configurations, availability, and dependencies. Improper changes may introduce vulnerabilities or downtime.

items.name: Allow lists / Deny lists
items.description: Controls that define which resources are permitted or blocked.

items.name: Restricted activities
items.description: Limiting actions during change processes to reduce risk.

items.name: Downtime
items.description: Period when systems are unavailable due to changes.

items.name: Legacy applications
items.description: Older systems that may not support modern changes.

items.name: Dependencies
items.description: Systems or services that rely on each other.

quiz.question: What can happen if dependencies are ignored?
Restarting a service after change is necessary to:
quiz.explanation: Changes require service restart to take effect.

topics.title: Documentation
topics.content: Proper documentation ensures that all changes are tracked and systems remain understandable and secure.

items.name: Updating diagrams
items.description: Ensuring system architecture diagrams reflect current state.

items.name: Updating policies/procedures
items.description: Updating documentation to align with new changes.

topics.title: Version Control
topics.content: Version control tracks changes to code and configurations, ensuring traceability and rollback capability.

items.name: Version control
items.description: System to track and manage changes in files and configurations.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of version control?
Which tool is used for version control?
quiz.explanation: Git is a version control system.

topics.title: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)
topics.content: PKI is a framework used to manage digital certificates and encryption keys to ensure secure communication, authentication, and trust between entities.

items.name: Public key
items.description: A key that can be shared publicly and is used to encrypt data or verify digital signatures.

items.name: Private key
items.description: A secret key used to decrypt data or create digital signatures.

items.name: Key escrow
items.description: Secure storage of cryptographic keys by a third party for recovery purposes.

quiz.question: Which key must remain secret?
quiz.explanation: Private keys must never be exposed.

topics.title: Encryption
topics.content: Encryption ensures confidentiality by converting plaintext into ciphertext. It can be applied at different levels and for different use cases.

quiz.question: Which encryption is used for data in transit?
Which encryption is best for disk protection?

topics.title: Cryptographic Tools
topics.content: Hardware and software tools used to secure cryptographic operations and protect keys.

items.name: Trusted Platform Module (TPM)
items.description: Hardware chip that securely stores cryptographic keys.

items.name: Hardware Security Module (HSM)
items.description: Dedicated device for secure key management.

items.name: Key management system
items.description: System to generate and manage keys.

topics.title: Obfuscation
topics.content: Techniques used to hide or disguise data without full encryption.

topics.title: Hashing & Related
topics.content: Hashing ensures data integrity using one-way functions.

topics.title: Blockchain
topics.content: A decentralized ledger used to securely record transactions.

topics.title: Certificates
topics.content: Certificates are used to bind identities to public keys and establish trust.

topics.title: Threat Actors
topics.content: Threat actors are individuals or groups that carry out malicious activities against systems or organizations.

items.name: Nation-state
items.description: Government-sponsored actors conducting cyber operations for political, military, or economic objectives.

items.name: Unskilled attacker
items.description: Individuals with little technical knowledge using pre-built tools.

items.name: Hacktivist
items.description: Actors motivated by political or ideological beliefs.

items.name: Insider threat
items.description: Trusted individuals who misuse access intentionally or unintentionally.

items.name: Organized crime
items.description: Highly structured groups focused on financial gain.

items.name: Shadow IT
items.description: Unauthorized systems or applications used within an organization.

quiz.question: Which threat actor is most likely to have high funding and resources?
quiz.explanation: Nation-states have significant resources and funding.

quiz.question: A script kiddie is:
quiz.explanation: They use pre-made tools without deep understanding.

quiz.question: Which actor is most likely to launch ransomware for profit?

topics.title: Attributes of Actors
topics.content: Threat actors can be classified based on their origin, resources, and level of sophistication.

items.name: Internal / External
items.description: Internal actors operate within the organization, while external actors attack from outside.

items.name: Resources / Funding
items.description: The amount of financial and technological resources available.

items.name: Level of sophistication
items.description: The skill level and technical capability of the attacker.

quiz.question: Which actor type has the highest level of sophistication?
An employee accessing sensitive data is:
Which factor determines attack complexity?
quiz.explanation: More skilled attackers perform complex attacks.

topics.title: Motivations
topics.content: Threat actors are driven by different motivations that influence their targets and attack methods.

items.name: Blackmail
items.description: Threatening to release data unless paid.

quiz.question: A hacktivist attack is driven by:
An employee leaking data due to anger is motivated by:

topics.title: Message-based
topics.content: Attackers use messaging platforms to deliver malicious content or deceive users into performing unsafe actions.

items.name: Email
items.description: Common vector used to deliver phishing attacks or malicious attachments.

items.name: Short Message Service (SMS)
items.description: Used for smishing attacks with malicious links.

items.name: Instant messaging (IM)
items.description: Messaging apps used to spread malicious links or impersonate users.

quiz.question: Which vector is most commonly used for phishing?
quiz.explanation: Email is the most common phishing vector.

topics.title: File and Image-based
topics.content: Attackers embed malicious code inside files or images to exploit vulnerabilities.

items.name: File-based
items.description: Malicious files such as PDFs or executables used to infect systems.

items.name: Image-based
items.description: Malware hidden in image files or exploiting image parsing vulnerabilities.

quiz.question: Malicious PDF is an example of:
quiz.explanation: It is delivered as a file.

topics.title: Other Vectors
topics.content: Various attack vectors that rely on physical devices or communication methods.

items.name: Voice call
items.description: Attack via phone calls to manipulate users.

items.name: Removable device
items.description: Malicious USB devices used to infect systems.

topics.title: Vulnerable Software
topics.content: Software vulnerabilities can be exploited by attackers.

topics.title: Unsecure Networks
topics.content: Insecure networks can expose systems to attacks.

items.name: Wired
items.description: Physical networks can be tapped or misconfigured.

quiz.question: Which network is most vulnerable to eavesdropping?

topics.title: Misconfigurations
topics.content: Improper configurations increase attack surface.

items.name: Default credentials
items.description: Default usernames and passwords left unchanged.

topics.title: Supply Chain
topics.content: Third-party providers can introduce risks into systems.

topics.title: Human Vectors / Social Engineering
topics.content: Social engineering exploits human behavior to gain access or information.

items.name: Pretexting
items.description: Creating a fake scenario to obtain information.

items.name: Typosquatting
items.description: Fake domains similar to real ones.

topics.title: Application
topics.content: Application vulnerabilities originate from insecure coding practices or logic flaws.

items.name: Memory injection
items.description: Injecting malicious code into process memory.

items.name: Buffer overflow
items.description: Writing data beyond buffer limits causing memory corruption.

items.name: Time-of-check (TOC)
items.description: Validation occurs before use, allowing manipulation.

items.name: Time-of-use (TOU)
items.description: System state changes between check and execution.

topics.title: Operating System (OS)-based
topics.content: Vulnerabilities related to flaws or misconfigurations in operating systems.

items.name: OS vulnerabilities
items.description: Weaknesses in OS design or unpatched systems.

topics.title: Web-based
topics.content: Web application vulnerabilities targeting browsers or web servers.

items.name: SQL injection (SQLi)
items.description: Injecting malicious SQL queries into input fields.

topics.title: Hardware
topics.content: Physical device vulnerabilities that can be exploited.

items.name: Firmware
items.description: Low-level software that may contain vulnerabilities.

items.name: End-of-life
items.description: Devices no longer supported with updates.

topics.title: Virtualization
topics.content: Vulnerabilities in virtual environments.

items.name: VM escape
items.description: Breaking out of a VM to access host system.

items.name: Resource reuse
items.description: Improper cleanup of resources shared between VMs.

topics.title: Cloud-specific
topics.content: Vulnerabilities unique to cloud environments.

topics.title: Supply Chain
topics.content: Vulnerabilities introduced by third-party components.

topics.title: Cryptographic
topics.content: Weaknesses in cryptographic implementations.

topics.title: Misconfiguration
topics.content: Improper system configuration creating vulnerabilities.

topics.title: Mobile Device
topics.content: Vulnerabilities specific to mobile devices.

topics.title: Zero-day
topics.content: Unknown vulnerabilities with no available patch.

items.name: Zero-day
items.description: New vulnerability not yet known or patched.

topics.title: Malware attacks
topics.content: Malware refers to malicious software designed to harm systems or steal data.

items.name: Virus
items.description: Attaches to files and spreads when executed.

items.name: Rootkit
items.description: Hides malicious activity and maintains persistence.

quiz.question: Encrypted files and ransom note indicate:
Which malware hides itself deeply in system?

topics.title: Physical attacks
topics.content: Attacks involving physical access or environmental manipulation.

items.name: Brute force
items.description: Trying many combinations to gain access.

topics.title: Network attacks
topics.content: Attacks targeting network infrastructure or communication.

items.name: Amplified
items.description: Using third-party servers to increase attack traffic.

items.name: Reflected
items.description: Spoofing victim IP to redirect traffic.

items.name: Malicious code
items.description: Injecting harmful code in network traffic.

topics.title: Application attacks
topics.content: Attacks targeting application logic or input handling.

topics.title: Cryptographic attacks
topics.content: Attacks targeting cryptographic systems.

items.name: Collision
items.description: Finding two inputs with same hash.

topics.title: Password attacks
topics.content: Attacks targeting authentication credentials.

items.name: Password spraying
items.description: Trying common passwords across many accounts.

quiz.question: Trying one password on many users is:

topics.title: Indicators
topics.content: Signs that indicate potential malicious activity.

items.name: Impossible travel
items.description: Logins from distant locations in short time.

quiz.question: Login from two countries within minutes indicates:
High CPU usage unexpectedly may indicate:

topics.title: Segmentation
topics.content: Segmentation divides networks into smaller zones to limit the spread of attacks.

topics.title: Access Control
topics.content: Mechanisms used to restrict access to systems and data.

topics.title: Application Allow List
topics.content: Only approved applications are allowed to run.

topics.title: Isolation
topics.content: Separating systems to prevent interaction.

topics.title: Patching
topics.content: Updating systems to fix vulnerabilities.

topics.title: Encryption
topics.content: Protecting data confidentiality.

topics.title: Monitoring
topics.content: Tracking system activity to detect threats.

topics.title: Least Privilege
topics.content: Users get minimum access required.

topics.title: Configuration Enforcement
topics.content: Ensuring systems follow secure configurations.

topics.title: Decommissioning
topics.content: Securely removing systems from use.

topics.title: Hardening Techniques
topics.content: Reducing system attack surface.

topics.title: Architecture and Infrastructure Concepts
topics.content: Different architecture models impact security, scalability, and operational risk.

items.name: Cloud
items.description: Shared responsibility model between provider and customer.

items.name: Responsibility matrix
items.description: Defines who is responsible for security controls.

items.name: Hybrid
items.description: Combination of cloud and on-prem systems.

items.name: Serverless
items.description: No server management, code runs on demand.

items.name: Virtualization
items.description: Running multiple OS on one system.

quiz.question: Who is responsible for security in cloud?
quiz.explanation: Both provider and customer share responsibilities.

topics.title: Considerations
topics.content: Key factors when choosing architecture models.

topics.title: Infrastructure Considerations
topics.content: Security depends on proper infrastructure design, placement, and control selection.

items.name: Device placement
items.description: Strategic positioning of devices to control traffic.

items.name: Attack surface
items.description: Total exposure points available to attackers.

items.name: Connectivity
items.description: How systems communicate with each other.

items.name: Inline
items.description: Device placed directly in traffic path.

quiz.question: Which mode blocks traffic if system fails?

topics.title: Network Appliances
topics.content: Security devices used to protect infrastructure.

items.name: Jump server
items.description: Secure entry point for administrative access.

items.name: IDS
items.description: Detects attacks but does not block.

topics.title: Port Security
topics.content: Mechanisms to secure network access points.

topics.title: Firewall Types
topics.content: Different firewalls provide varying levels of protection.

items.name: Layer 4
items.description: Filters based on IP and port.

topics.title: Secure Communication and Access
topics.content: Techniques to secure remote access and communication.

topics.title: Selection of Effective Controls
topics.content: Choosing the right controls based on risk and environment.

topics.title: Data Types
topics.content: Different types of data require different protection levels based on sensitivity and legal requirements.

items.name: Regulated
items.description: Data protected by laws and regulations.

quiz.question: Which data is protected by law?

topics.title: Data Classifications
topics.content: Classification defines the sensitivity level of data.

items.name: Sensitive
items.description: Requires protection but not highly restricted.

topics.title: Data States and Considerations
topics.content: Data security depends on its state and location.

topics.title: Methods to Secure Data
topics.content: Techniques used to protect data from unauthorized access.

topics.title: High Availability
topics.content: Ensuring systems remain operational with minimal downtime.

items.name: Clustering
items.description: Group of systems working together for redundancy.

quiz.question: Clustering provides:
quiz.explanation: Ensures availability if one node fails.

topics.title: Site Considerations
topics.content: Different recovery site types based on readiness and cost.

items.name: Warm site
items.description: Partially ready with some setup needed.

quiz.question: Which site is fastest to recover?

topics.title: Platform Diversity
topics.content: Using different systems to reduce common failure risk.

topics.title: Multi-cloud Systems
topics.content: Using multiple cloud providers for resilience.

topics.title: Continuity of Operations
topics.content: Maintaining business functions during disruptions.

topics.title: Capacity Planning
topics.content: Planning resources to handle workloads.

topics.content: Validating recovery plans.

topics.content: Ensuring data recovery after loss.

topics.content: Ensuring systems remain powered during outages.

topics.content: Different systems require specific hardening techniques.

topics.content: Proper planning is required for secure wireless deployment.

topics.title: Mobile Solutions
topics.content: Managing and securing mobile devices.

topics.content: Configurations to secure wireless networks.

topics.content: Observing systems to detect anomalies.

topics.content: Tracking and monitoring assets throughout their lifecycle.

topics.content: Evaluating and prioritizing identified vulnerabilities.

topics.content: Documenting vulnerabilities and remediation status.

topics.content: Continuous observation of systems, applications, and infrastructure to detect abnormal behavior.

items.description: Generating alerts when suspicious events occur.

topics.content: Using secure protocols and configurations.

topics.content: Using multiple authentication factors.

topics.content: Automation reduces manual work and enforces security processes consistently.
